segments.s576-g65: همه خواستند آن زمان زینهار: Subject (همه, “everyone”) + past verb (خواستند, “they asked/wished”). آن زمان places the action in time (“at that time”). زینهار is an interjection/noun here — a plea or admonition (literally “beware” / “take heed”) used as what they asked for (i.e. “spare us” / “have mercy”). Word order is typical Persian: subject → verb, with temporal phrase and object/quote inserted.
segments.s576-g66: نوشتند نامه‌بر شهریار: نوشتند is the past-tense plural of نوشتن (“they wrote”). نامه‌بر is a compound meaning the bearer/messenger of a letter (the means by which the letter is sent). شهریار is the indirect object/target — “to the king.” The clause literally states they wrote (and dispatched) a letter by a messenger to the king.
segments.s576-g67: ببخشیدشان نامبردار شاه: ببخشیدشان is a past-tense/perfective verb phrase meaning “he pardoned/forgave them” (the -شان suffix marks the object “them”). نامبردار modifies شاه and here is an honorific/descriptor “the renowned/aforementioned.” The normal Persian order (verb before modifier) is inverted poetically but the meaning is straightforward: the king forgave them.
segments.s576-g68: بفرمود تا بازگردد سپاه: بفرمود is an honorific past verb “he commanded/said” (courtly diction). تا introduces a subordinate clause of result/command here (“that / so that”), so تا + بازگردد (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive-like form used after تا) yields “that the army return.” سپاه is the subject of بازگردد (“the army”).
segments.s576-g69: به هر کشوری نامداری گرفت: به هر کشوری is a locative phrase (“in/to each country/realm”) with به used poetically to mark the domain. هر = “every.” نامداری is the abstract noun “renown/fame.” گرفت is past tense “he took/obtained” — here “he acquired.” The verb follows the object phrase, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s576-g70: همان بر جهان کامگاری گرفت: همان (“that same/thereby”) picks up what was just mentioned (his renown). بر جهان = “over the world” (بر as a preposition “upon/over”). کامگاری is a poetic noun meaning “fulfillment/success,” and گرفت again is “he obtained/gained.” The clause says that by that fame he achieved success across the world.
segments.s576-g71: همی خواندندیش پیروز شاه: همی is an adverbial particle meaning ongoing/continuous (“kept / continually”). خواندندیش is a contracted/poetic form meaning “they called him” / “they named him” (the clitic ایش/یش refers to ‘him’). پیروز شاه is the title placed after the verb phrase — “Victorious King.” The clause is a continuous/habitual statement: they continually addressed him as the Victorious King.
segments.s576-g72: همی بود یک چند با تاج و گاه: همی بود = “he was / remained” (ongoing past). یک چند is an idiomatic phrase meaning “for a short while / for some time.” با تاج و گاه literally “with crown and gāh”; گاه in poetry can mean “place/seat” (i.e. throne) or more generally “time/occasion,” so the phrase describes him being for a time in possession of the crown (and seat/throne). Word order is subject-less descriptive: verb + duration phrase + accompaniment.
segments.s576-g41: همی is an archaic/progressive particle placed before the verb to mark continued or habitual action; رفت is the past-tense verb 'went'. شادان is an adverbial/adjunct of manner 'joyfully' following the verb. به + place (اصطخر پارس) is a prepositional phrase indicating destination.
segments.s576-g42: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'); اصطخر is the subject. بد here is an archaic orthography for بُد (بود) = 'was' (past of بودن) rather than the adjective 'bad'. بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the earth' and فخر پارس is the predicate nominal 'the pride/glory of Pars'.
segments.s576-g43: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate temporal clause; اندر is the locative preposition 'in/at'. نصیبین here functions as a place name (Nisibis). خبر یافتند is a past-tense verbal phrase 'they received/learned (the) news'.
segments.s576-g44: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; جنگ را uses the object/definiteness marker را but here marks the goal/directed event 'the battle'. تیز is an adverbial 'swift(ly)' modifying the verb, and بشتافتند is the past tense 'they hurried / hastened'. Word order is subject + (marked) object/goal + adverb + verb.
segments.s576-g45: که begins an explanatory/subordinate clause; ما را نباید is an impersonal construction meaning 'it is not right/proper for us' (literally 'for us it should not be'). The following که introduces the content clause that names the feared action; شاپور شاه is the subject-title 'Shapur the king'.
segments.s576-g46: نصیبین is the place-object (Nisibis) expected to be taken; بگیرد is a present/subjunctive-form of گرفتْن 'to seize/take' expressing the potential/action feared. بیارد (here a verb 'bring') parallels بگیرد and takes سپاه 'army' as its object; the sequence gives two coordinated actions ('take Nisibis and bring an army').
segments.s576-g47: که again introduces the reason/explanation. دینِ مسیحا is a genitive/construct phrase 'the religion/faith of the Messiah' = Christianity. ندارد درست is a negation 'does not have [being] correct' i.e. 'is not true/correct' with ندارد carrying the negative existential/possessive sense and درست as the predicate adjective.
segments.s576-g48: همش 'all of it' is the subject phrase; کیش is 'creed/sect' and زردشت و زند is a compound predicate nominal ('of Zoroaster and the Zand'). است is the copula 'is'; the repeated و است at the end is a poetic/dialectal emphatic restatement ('and so it is').
segments.s576-g9: همه مهتران is the subject (‘all the chiefs/elders’). نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the presence of / before the king’, and آمدند is a 3rd-person-plural past tense verb (‘they came’). Word order is Subject + prepositional phrase + verb, the normal Persian order for a past-tense clause.
segments.s576-g10: برهنه سر و بی‌کلاه is a coordinated manner phrase describing how they came: adjective + noun (برهنه سر = ‘bare-headed’) joined with و to another adjective (بی‌کلاه = ‘hatless’). آمدند is the same 3rd-person-plural past verb as before; the verb applies to the whole coordinated phrase. The order (adjective before head noun) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s576-g11: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning ‘when’. دینار is the noun ‘dinar(s)’, and پیشش (‘before him / in his presence’) indicates the location/direction of the action. فرو + ریختند is a compound verb (فرو as a directional/intensive prefix + ریختند, 3rd-pl. past of ریختن) meaning ‘they poured/they cast down’ or ‘were thrown down’ in poetic usage.
segments.s576-g12: بگسترده qualifies the following noun phrase and functions adverbially (‘spread out/widely’): it has the old prefix بـ attached to an adjective. زر کهن is the noun phrase ‘old/ancient gold’. بیختند is the past verb of the clause, 3rd-person-plural; here it functions as the main predicate (‘they scattered/laid out’), though the exact verbal form is an archaic/poetic variant.
segments.s576-g13: ببخشود is a 3rd-person-singular past of ببخشیدن here meaning ‘he granted/bestowed’ (an act of giving). The conjunction و links this action to the next: شاپور (the name) is the subject of the following verb, and بنواختشان is 3rd-singular past with the object clitic شان (‘for them’), meaning ‘Shapur played/struck (music) for them’. The prefixed بـ before the verb and the enclitic شان are typical of older literary Persian.
segments.s576-g14: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘with goodness / kindly / well’, where به functions as an adverbial/prepositional particle and خوبی is the abstract noun ‘goodness/kindness’. بر اندازه is another prepositional phrase meaning ‘according to measure / fittingly’, with بر indicating ‘according to / upon’ and اندازه = ‘measure/extent’. بنشاختشان is a past verb with the object clitic شان (‘he set/seated/placed them’); this verb form uses the archaic prefix بـ plus past stem and takes شان as its object.
segments.s576-g15: برانوش را گفت is ‘he said/told (it) to Branoush’; را here marks Branoush as the indirect object (the person addressed). گفت is the past tense verb ‘said/told’. کز شهر روم is a fused relative/prepositional expression (کز = که + از, ‘who/from’) introducing that/whom/from the city of Rome, so کز شهر روم = ‘who came from the city of Rome’.
segments.s576-g16: بیامد is 3rd-person-singular past ‘came’. بسی here is a classical adverb meaning ‘many / much’ modifying مرد (‘men’). The adjectives that follow — بیداد and شوم — modify مرد (‘cruel / oppressive’ and ‘ill-omened / unlucky’), and the conjunction و links those two descriptors. The adjective sequence appears after the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s576-g89: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb to mean 'thus/so.' گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' with an omitted third-person subject (typical in Persian). کاینست is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'که این است' = 'this is/that is,' and فرجام ما is a genitival phrase 'our end' (فرجام = end/fate, ما = our). Together: 'Thus he said: this is our end.'
segments.s576-g90: ندانم is the negative present form 'I do not know' (ن + دانم). کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where.' باشد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here to express uncertainty about location ('may be / will be / is'); آرام ما is a noun + possessive 'our rest/peace.' The clause reads: 'I do not know where our repose will be.'
segments.s576-g91: یکی را picks out one person for emphasis (یکی = 'one/someone'; را here a topical/emphatic marker). همه functions adverbially as 'altogether/entirely.' زفتی is an adjective 'nasty/vile.' ابلهیست is a fused form where ابلهی (foolishness) + یست/است yields 'is foolish' (poetic predicate). The overall sense: 'One (person) is entirely nasty and foolish.'
segments.s576-g92: یکی again 'one/another.' با introduces the attribute 'with' (با خردمندی = 'with wisdom'). خردمندی is a noun meaning 'wisdom/prudence.' و links the two predicates; فرهیست is an archaic/poetic predicate 'is learned/illustrious' (فرهی + یست/است). The clause means: 'Another is with wisdom and is learned.'
segments.s576-g93: برین و بران is a poetic pair meaning 'on this and on that' (بر + این/آن contracted), i.e. 'this way and that.' روز هم بگذرد places the noun 'day' before the verb; بگذرد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive/present-factual 'passes/will pass.' هم adds 'also/even.' Together: 'This day and that day too will pass.'
segments.s576-g94: خنگ is an adjectival noun 'stupid/foolish.' آنک is a relative pronoun 'that/who.' گیتی means 'the world.' به بد = 'to the bad/wicked.' نسپرد is the past stem سپرد (to entrust) with the negative prefix ن = 'did not entrust/hand over.' The clause reads: 'Foolish is he who did not entrust the world to the wicked.' (poetic inversion: subject implied by آنک).
segments.s576-g95: به تخت کیان = 'onto the throne of the Kayan (the Kayanid dynasty).' اندر آورد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'brought in/placed' (here idiomatically 'set [one's] foot'). پای 'foot' completes the idiom پای آوردن بر تخت = 'to set foot on the throne' = 'to ascend the throne.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s576-g96: همی بود is an imperfective/continuous past 'he was / he remained.' چندی is a durative adverb 'for a while.' جهان کدخدای is a poetic genitive/inversion meaning 'headman (کدخدای) of the world (جهان)' i.e. 'ruler of the world.' Together: 'He remained, for a while, the world's headman.'
segments.s576-g1: 'برانوش' is the proper name (subject). 'چون' functions as a temporal conjunction here meaning “when/as”. 'پاسخ‌نامه' is the object “the reply/response letter,” and 'دید' is the past-tense verb “saw/ beheld.” Word order: Name + temporal conjunction + object + past verb; the clause sets the time for what follows.
segments.s576-g2: 'ز' is the poetic preposition “from/with,” marking cause (here: from joy). 'شادی' is the cause “joy.” 'دل پاک‌تن' is a compound noun phrase “pure-bodied/pure-hearted heart/soul” (دل = heart; پاک‌تن = pure-bodied/pure-hearted). 'بردمید' is a poetic past-form meaning to be carried away/swept up (literally ‘was carried off’); it is third-person singular past and links as the main predicate saying the heart was carried away by joy.
segments.s576-g3: 'بفرمود' is an honorific past verb meaning he commanded/said (polite narrative past). 'تا' introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here (“so that/that”). 'نامداران روم' is the noun phrase “the renowned/illustrious men of Rome” and is the content of the order. The verb of the subordinate clause follows in the next segment.
segments.s576-g4: 'برفتند' is the past plural verb “they departed/went.” 'صد مرد' is the quantifier plus noun “a hundred men.” 'زان' is the ablative demonstrative “from that,” and 'مرز و بوم' is the coordinated phrase “border and homeland/land.” The clause thus states where the hundred men departed from.
segments.s576-g5: 'درم' is the material being loaded (“dirhams,” i.e., money). 'بار' here means “a load/cargo.” 'کردند' is the past plural auxiliary/verb “they did/they made” used with 'بار' to mean “they loaded.” 'خروار' is a large-quantity noun modifier (“a great amount”); 'شست' in the manuscript looks like an item/measure but none of the provided senses fit exactly, so its exact sense is uncertain in the given choices (marked -1). Overall: they piled up large amounts of money.
segments.s576-g6: 'هم' means “also/even.” 'از' is the preposition “from/of,” introducing the items. 'گوهر و جامه' are the listed goods “gems and garments.” The phrase 'بر نشست' is a compound where 'بر' (on/upon) combines with the past verb 'نشست' (sat/settled/was set down) to mean “were placed/set down”; syntactically the items precede the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s576-g7: The opening 'ز' is the poetic preposition “from/of.” 'دینار گنجی' is a genitive phrase “a treasure of dinars” (money-treasure). The following 'ز' takes the causal/for-purpose sense (“for/because of”) and combines with 'بهر' meaning “for/for the sake of”; 'نثار' means “offering/dedication.” Together the phrase says a treasure of dinars was provided for the purpose of gifts/offerings.
segments.s576-g8: 'فراز آمد' is a poetic verbal phrase meaning “rose up/was brought forth/appeared” (verb before source phrase, as often in classical Persian). 'از هر سوی' is a prepositional phrase “from every side/direction.” The numeral phrase 'سی هزار' (thirty thousand) follows to quantify what came. Overall: thirty thousand (units/people/treasures) came from every direction.
segments.s576-g17: به ایران‌زمین is a locative prepositional phrase ('to/into the land of Iran' used here as 'in the land of Iran'). آنچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which/whatever', introducing the noun phrase that follows; بد modifies شارستان as an adjective ('bad, ruined town'). Word order is classical: prepositional locative + relative marker + adjectival phrase describing the towns.
segments.s576-g18: کنون is an adverb 'now'. گشت is the past/resultive verb 'became/has become' (classical usage often uses a singular verb even for an implied plural/collective antecedent like آنچ). یکسر and همه are adverbial intensifiers ('entirely' / 'all'), and خارستان is the predicate noun 'thorny thicket' — together: 'has now altogether become thorny thickets'.
segments.s576-g19: عوض here functions as 'in exchange/compensation' (introducing the speaker's remedial action). خواهم is the present/future auxiliary 'I will' (desire/intent), taking the following clause as its object. آن را is the definite object ('that thing' + object marker را), and که introduces the relative clause 'that has been ruined' (ویران شدست). شدست is an archaic contracted perfect/resultive ('has become/has been') equivalent to modern شده‌است.
segments.s576-g20: کنام is a locative noun 'in the bosom/at the side of' used figuratively as 'in the haunt of'. پلنگان و شیران is the coordinated subject/noun phrase 'leopards and lions'. شدست again is the archaic/resultive verb 'has become' — so: 'it has become the lair/haunt of leopards and lions.'
segments.s576-g21: برانوش گفتا = 'Branush said' (گفتا is the archaic past 'said'). چه باید بگوی is an interrogative clause 'what must I say?' — باید is the modal 'must/should', and بگوی is an archaic or truncated subjunctive form meaning roughly 'I should say / I ought to say' (classical short form for بگویم); the exact verb sense isn't provided in the listed senses, so it is marked as unmatched.
segments.s576-g22: چو = 'when/since'. زنهار دادی is 'you gave a warning' (دادی = 'you gave'). مه here is a poetic word 'the moon' used metaphorically (often for one’s own beauty or self) — in this clause it functions as the thing affected by the warning. بر تاب is from the verbal expression برتابیدن ('to endure / to bear'), where بر (on/upon) + تاب ('endurance') form the idea 'to endure', and روی is 'face' (object of the notion 'to look at / endure looking at'). The whole clause: 'when you gave a warning (that) [the moon / I] should not endure / should not look upon your face.'
segments.s576-g23: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave an answer' (داد used as 'uttered/gave'). گرانمایه شاه is a descriptive noun phrase 'the esteemed/noble king' (گرانمایه = 'esteemed/precious' modifying شاه). Word order: formulaic adverb + verb + object, then the epithet + noun.
segments.s576-g24: چو here functions conditionally 'if'. خواهی is 2nd-person present 'you wish/you want' and که introduces the subordinate clause. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely', and ببخشم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present of بخشیدن ('that I forgive / I may forgive'), used here as the speaker's conditional/future action: 'if you want me to forgive the sin entirely.' گناه is the object 'sin'.
segments.s576-g81: به روم اندرون here is a prepositional locative phrase with a slightly archaic word order: به + place + اندرون = 'in the interior of Rome'. هرچ بودش is a relative/indefinite clause 'whatever there was' where بودش is بود + enclitic -ش referring back to the thing (the treasure). ز گنج attaches to هرچ بودش and specifies that the 'whatever' consists of treasure ('of treasure' / 'treasures that were there').
segments.s576-g82: فراز آوریده is a perfect/participial idea 'having brought up / hauled up on high' (فراز used adverbially/locatively). ز هر سو 'from every side/quarter' gives the source/direction, and به رنج 'with hardship' expresses the manner/effort. The whole segment modifies or completes the previous phrase, describing how the treasures were brought.
segments.s576-g83: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought'. و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. یکسر here modifies the giving and means 'entirely / altogether / all at once'. به شاپور داد is a ditransitive structure 'gave to Shapur' with به introducing the indirect object and داد the past 'gave'. Word order places the verb بیاورد before the coordination and the verb داد completes the clause.
segments.s576-g84: همی بود is a combination where همی = 'still / was still' and بود is the past copula ('there was / remained'). یک چند in this context functions adverbially as 'for a short while' or 'for a little time'. لب here is used as 'brim' or 'edge' (not literal mouth), and پر ز باد = 'filled with wind' or 'fluttering with wind' describing the state of that brim. The clause therefore gives a brief comment about the hat's condition.
segments.s576-g85: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally / in the end'. در بند و زندان is a doubled locative expression ('in fetters and in prison' — بند as 'bond/fetter' plus زندان 'prison') giving place/state. بمرد is an older-form past/jussive verb often used in narrative to mean 'he died' (literally an optative/jussive form but here narratively translated as past).
segments.s576-g86: کلاه کیی is a noun phrase 'the Kayan (kingly) hat' (کلاه = hat, کیی = Kayanian/pertaining to the Kayan kings). دیگری را سپرد is a transitive clause: دیگری را (with را as the definite object marker marking 'another' as the object) and سپرد (past of سپردن) 'entrusted / handed over'. The verb takes را-marked object and indicates transfer to 'another'.
segments.s576-g87: به رومش places the destination fronted ('to Rome' with the enclitic -ش = 'his' or 'its' attached to روم). فرستاد is the past verb 'sent'. شاپور شاه follows as the nominative subject in the emphatic position 'Shapur the king'. The sentence thus uses flexible word order (object/directional phrase before verb, subject after) typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s576-g88: به تابوت is a locative/ablative phrase 'in/into a coffin' (here indicating how it was sent). وز is an archaic linking particle here glossed roughly as 'and/with' connecting تابوت and مشک; مشک 'musk' names the perfume placed with the object. بر سر کلاه is a locative phrase 'on the head of the hat' (i.e. placed atop the hat). Together they describe the manner/condition in which the hat (or object) was sent.
segments.s576-g57: همی گفت is a literary progressive/past: همی (an older continuous adverb) + گفت (past) → 'he kept saying'. پیغمبری here is a noun phrase 'a prophet' (used as the topic/subject of the reported clause). کش is an agentive/relational element attached before the object جهود, forming a compound meaning 'killer of / one who acts against' and efforts to mark the relationship to جهود (an archaic plural 'Jews'). Word order is topic ('a prophet') followed by the descriptive particle + target ('کش جهود').
segments.s576-g58: کشد is the verb of the embedded clause (present/subjunctive/old-form expectation: 'he would / who would / who kills'); دین او را is the direct object with او ('their/his' in context — here referring to the Jews/them) and را as the definite object marker placed after the pronoun; نشاید ستود is a fixed literary construction: نشاید (it is not proper / should not) + ستود (to praise) → 'should not be praised'. The clause thus states the judgment about the prophet's action.
segments.s576-g59: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / like' (به + noun used adverbially), and گرد here means 'around/about' describing the movement — together 'set out circling in the manner of...'.
segments.s576-g60: This is a simile specifying how they moved: سواران 'horsemen' and شیران (poetic plural, 'lions' used figuratively for brave men) — و simply links the two nouns — روز نبرد 'the day of battle' is a genitival/time phrase where روز (day) modifies نبرد (battle) to situate the comparison. The phrase as a whole describes the army as like horsemen and lions on battle-day.
segments.s576-g61: به یک هفته is a temporal expression 'for a week' (به + measure). آنجا 'there' marks the location. همی جنگ بود uses همی + بود to give a continuous past sense 'there was continual fighting' — جنگ is the noun 'battle/ fighting' and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s576-g62: دران is an older fused prepositional phrase (در + آن) meaning 'in that'. شهر is 'city'. از here expresses cause/agency 'because of / from' (از جنگ = because of the fighting). بس is an intensifier here ('very' / 'much'), and تنگ بود is 'was tight/pressed' — together 'in that city things were very strained because of the war.'
segments.s576-g63: بکشتند is the past plural verb 'they killed/slain'. زیشان is a contracted/prepositional phrase meaning 'of them / from them' (indicating the victims). فراوان modifies the verb phrase as an adverb 'many/much'. سران can mean 'heads' literally or 'leaders' — here likely literal 'heads' or 'chiefs' taken in context.
segments.s576-g64: نهادند is the past plural 'they placed/put'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. زنده is 'living' (adjective). بند is 'fetter/bond' and گران 'heavy' modifies بند — together this phrase indicates that they set (something, implied: heads) upon the living, bound with heavy fetters; the object (heads) is understood from the previous segment and the verb نهادند governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s576-g73: کنیزک is the head noun 'the little maid/maidservant'. The following clause introduced by که is a relative clause modifying کنیزک; او را is the definite object (را marks it), رهانیده is a past participle 'freed' and بود functions as the auxiliary forming the past perfect/ pluperfect ('had freed'). The relative clause therefore reads 'who had freed him'.
segments.s576-g74: بدان here is the prepositional compound (ب + آن = 'to/into that') meaning 'to that', followed by the noun کامگاری 'fulfillment/success'. رسانیده is the past participle 'caused to reach / brought (to)', and بود is the auxiliary forming the pluperfect 'had brought (to)'. Together: 'had brought (him) to that fulfillment.' (Note: دان is a segmentation artefact here and functions as part of بدان = 'to that'.)
segments.s576-g75: دلفروزو فرخ‌پیش is a sequence of epithets/names (literally 'heart-pleasing, fortunate-front') placed before the light verb phrase 'نام کرد'. نام here is the noun 'name' used in the idiom نام کرد 'gave the name (called)', and کرد is the past tense of 'to do/make' functioning as the light verb 'called'. The explicit object ('her') appears in the next segment, so the verb's object is postponed (a common poetic word order).
segments.s576-g76: ز is the preposition 'from/among' (here 'among'), خوبان 'the beauties' (i.e. the beautiful ones), مر is the archaic dative/pronominal 'to me' ('for me' / 'my'), او را is the definite object 'her' (را marking the object), دلارام functions as a proper/name or epithet 'Delaram', and کرد completes the clause 'made (her)'. Grammatically this says 'among the beauties he made her my Delaram' — note the older placement of the dative pronoun (مر) before the object phrase.
segments.s576-g77: همان باغبان را places 'that very gardener' as a topical/definite object (را marking topicalization). بسی is an adverb 'much/greatly', and خواسته is a past participle/compound verbal adjective 'sought/wanted' (literally 'much-wanted'). Together they mean 'that same gardener was greatly sought/desired.'
segments.s576-g78: This is a coordinated sequence of verbs/actions joined by و. بداد here is ambiguous in the supplied sense list (the expected reading in context is a past-tense verb 'gave' or 'granted' rather than the listed noun 'oppression'); گسی is an indefinite 'someone/some' that serves as the (indefinite) subject of the following verb, کردش is 'did/made him' with the enclitic -ش referring back to the gardener, and آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned'. So the clause reads roughly 'they granted (it), and someone made him adorned.'
segments.s576-g79: همی بود is the imperfective/past-continuous idiom 'was still / remained'. قیصر is the subject 'Caesar', and به زندان و بند is a locative/condition phrase 'in prison and fetters' (به + noun indicating state/location). The verb بود is the past copula 'was', so the segment states Caesar's ongoing state of captivity.
segments.s576-g80: A prepositional list introduced by به applies to the preceding clause: زاری 'lamentation', خواری 'humiliation/abasement', and زخم کمند 'wound of the snare' (zakhm + a following noun in attributive relation). The single به governs the series of nouns in a poetic asyndeton ('in lamentation and abasement and wound of the snare').
segments.s576-g97: وزان پس is an emphatic temporal phrase: وزان ('from that / from that time') + پس ('then/after'), placed before the following prepositional phrase. بر کشور خوزیان is a standard preposition (بر) + noun phrase (کشور 'realm/country' + خوزیان, the people/name 'the Khuzis'), so the whole segment means 'from that time on to the realm of the Khuzis.'
segments.s576-g98: فرستاد is a past-tense transitive verb (he sent/dispatched). It takes the following nouns as direct objects: بسیار ('many/much') modifies the paired nouns سود و زیان which are listed as the things sent — 'benefit' (سود) and 'loss' (زیان). The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s576-g99: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' or 'for'; ز here expresses reason/purpose and بهر reinforces 'for'. اسیران is the beneficiary ('the prisoners'). یکی شهر کرد is a causative construction: یکی ('one') + شهر ('city') + کرد (past of 'to make/do') = 'he made/established one city.'
segments.s576-g100: جهان را marks 'the world' with the object marker را (here the world is the thing affected). ازان بوم is a source phrase 'from that bōm/land' (ازان = 'from that'; بوم = 'homeland'). پر is a predicate/adjectival element meaning 'full (of)'; بهر کرد is a verbal phrase where بهر (here 'benefit/bounty') combines with کرد ('made/caused') to express 'caused to be full of bounty.' Together: 'he made the world full of bounty from that land.'
segments.s576-g101: کجا is a locative adverb 'where.' خرم‌آباد is a place-name (Khorramabad). بد نام is a postpositive adjective phrase 'bad-named' = 'of ill repute' (بد modifies نام = 'name/reputation'). شهر names the referent as a 'city' (appositive). The sequence describes the location and its reputation.
segments.s576-g102: وزان begins the clause ('and/from that'), linking back to the preceding reference. بوم خرم is the noun phrase 'the joyful/pleasant homeland' (بوم = 'homeland'; خرم = 'joyful/pleasant'). کرا is an archaic relative/particle here ('which' / 'that'), followed by بود (past copula 'was'). بهر at the end is a terse beneficiary/purpose marker ('for'/'to the benefit of'), a poetic elliptical closing meaning roughly 'which was a joyful abode for him.'
segments.s576-g103: کسی را که introduces a relative clause 'someone whom...' (کسی = 'someone'; را marks the person as definite/affected; که = 'who/that'). از پیش is an adverbial phrase 'previously / before.' ببرید is a perfective/past action of بردن here meaning 'you took/carry away' (in context 'had taken/captured'), and دست functions as the affected noun 'hand' or figuratively 'power/control' — together the clause describes 'someone whom you had taken earlier.'
segments.s576-g104: بدین is a fused prepositional/demonstrative expression 'to/into this' used adverbially here ('to this frontier'); it historically parses as ب ('to/into') + دین (archaic demonstrative). مرز is the noun 'border/frontier.' بودیش is a compact form meaning 'it belonged to him' (بودیش = بود + ایش 'to him' in older usage). جای نشست is a verbal phrase 'he took his seat / he settled in that place' (جای = 'place'; نشست = 'sat/settled').
segments.s576-g33: نوشتند is a third‑person plural past tense verb 'they wrote/made'; عهدی is its object 'a covenant/pledge'. ز + شاپور شاه is a source/instrumental phrase ('by/from Shapur the king') using poetic ز for modern از; word order (verb before object then source) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s576-g34: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' (referring back to the covenant) and links the clause to the previous segment. پس here has a temporal/sequence sense 'after/thereupon'. نراند is the verbal root راند with an initial ن that gives a negative/optative nuance here ('would not/send not drive'); ز ایران سپاه means 'an army from Iran' (ز = from). The clause thus expresses the result stipulated by the covenant.
segments.s576-g35: مگر functions as an exception/conditional 'unless' or 'except'; با introduces an instrumental/with phrase describing manner: 'with سزاواری و خرمی' = 'with due worthiness/fitness and gladness/delight'. The two nouns are coordinated by و.
segments.s576-g36: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. روم here is an archaic/subjunctive verbal form '(that) I go' (i.e. 'where shall I go'); this is an instance where the provided noun senses do not apply (see senses entry -1). را is the object/topic marker used for emphasis after the verb. ز + و (تogether 'زو' = از او) means 'from him'; نیاید is a negative/potential verb 'will not come' or 'will not be lacking'; کمی 'scarcity/little' gives the meaning 'there will be no lack' → 'I will not lack anything from him.'
segments.s576-g37: ازان پس = 'after that/thereupon' (ازان = 'from that; thereupon', پس = 'then'). گسی 'a certain one/someone' is the subject; کرد is the simple past 'did/acted'; بنواختشان is a transitive past form 'struck/played for them' (بنواخت + شان enclitic 'them'), here meaning someone began playing music for the company.
segments.s576-g38: سر (here 'head'/'top') with از نامداران ('from/of the renowned') forms an idiom meaning being set up or distinguished among the famed. برافراختشان is a past transitive 'he/she/it raised them aloft' with enclitic شان 'them' — i.e. he exalted them among the celebrated.
segments.s576-g39: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ایشان 'they' is the subject of برفتند, past plural 'they went/left'. لشکر is the next clause's subject 'the army'; براند (from راند) is a present/past-form verb 'sets in motion/drives' — here 'the army set out' (poetic word order places the verb after the subject).
segments.s576-g40: جهان‌آفرین 'the world‑creator' is the object (marked by را). فراوان is an adverb 'often/abundantly'. بخواند is a past/formal verb meaning here 'called upon / invoked / recited to' (in poetic contexts بخواند often means to call or to recite/praise), so the clause says he repeatedly invoked or praised the Creator.
segments.s576-g105: "بر و بوم او یکسر او را بدی" is a compact, slightly archaic phrasing: "بر و بوم او" (lit. "on and dwelling his") is an idiomatic compound meaning "his whole realm/land." "یکسر" is an adverb modifying the clause meaning "entirely/altogether." "او را بدی" has "او را" as the definite object (him) and "بدی" functioning as a nominal predicate meaning "evil/harm" (i.e. "did him harm"); the verb or agent is not made explicit here and is implied (the people/realm).
segments.s576-g106: "سر سال نو خلعتی بستدی" is a temporal phrase + object + verb. "سرِ سالِ نو" (= "at the head of the new year") means "at New Year's (day)"; "خلعتی" is the (indefinite) object "a robe/gift-robe"; "بستدی" is an archaic past form of بستن (here "to bind/tie/put on/fasten" or "to bestow/affix") in third person, so the clause means "they bound/put on/ bestowed a robe."
segments.s576-g107: "یکی شارستان کرد دیگر به شام" uses "یکی ... کرد" ("he made one ...") with the verb کرد distributed to the following phrase as well: "شارستان کرد" = "made (one) a town/city." "دیگر به شام" = "another (one) to the dinner/feast" where "به شام" means "for the evening/feast"—the verb is understood from the first clause.
segments.s576-g108: "که پیروز شاپور کردش به نام" is a relative clause introduced by "که" (= "which/that/who"). "پیروز شاپور" is an epithet + proper name ("the victorious Shapur"). "کردش" is the past-tense verb کرد with the enclitic object "-ش" ("him/it") attached, i.e. "he made him/it." "به نام" here means "by name/in name" (i.e. "made (him) known/famous by name").
segments.s576-g109: "به اهواز کرد آن سیم شارستان" places the action at a locale: "به اهواز کرد" = "(he) made/built at Ahvaz." "آن سیم شارستان" is a noun phrase "that (silver/fine) city" ("آن" = "that" + descriptive adjective implied by سیم), serving as the object of کرد. Classical Persian often puts the location phrase before the verb.
segments.s576-g110: "بدو اندرون کاخ و بیمارستان" continues the object/beneficiary information. "بدو" = "to him/for him/unto it" (clitic-like prepositional phrase indicating beneficiary), "اندرون" = "inside/within," followed by the coordinated nouns "کاخ و بیمارستان" (= "palace(s) and hospital"). The word order places the locative "اندرون" before the nouns it governs.
segments.s576-g111: "کنام اسیرانش کردند نام" uses "کنام" (a locative/positional word meaning literally "bosom/lap" or figuratively "beside/among") to set a sphere, then "اسیرانش" = "his captives" (اسیران + the possessive/enclitic -ش). "کردند نام" is a common classical phrasing meaning "they made/gave a name" (کردند = they did/made, نام = name); together: "they gave (a) name among/at the side of his captives."
segments.s576-g112: "اسیر اندرو یافتی خواب و کام" is a short clause: "اسیر" here reads as the state "in captivity" or "the captive," "اندرو" = "in it/in him/within that," and "یافتی" is the past form of یافتن "to find/obtain" in a classical form (here "he/it found/obtained"). The last two nouns "خواب و کام" = "sleep and fulfillment/satisfaction" are the things found. Word order is Subject/State + locative + verb + objects.
segments.s576-g25: This is an adverbial prepositional phrase giving source and frequency: ز + noun + adjective (ز دینار رومی) = 'from Roman dinars'. The following بــه سالی سه بار is an adverbial expression 'per year, three times' where به سالی ('per year') + عدد + بار ('three times') gives the frequency. Note classical word order: adjective رومی follows دینار.
segments.s576-g26: همی is a classical continuative/emphatic particle marking habitual or continuous action before the verb; داد here is the verb 'gave' (past/imperf. habitual). باید after the verb expresses necessity/obligation 'must / had to', and هزاران هزار is an emphatic plural 'thousands upon thousands'. The whole clause says he was obliged to give very large sums.
segments.s576-g27: دگر 'the other / the rest' introduces what remains; آنک is a relative 'that which / which', باشد here carries the sense 'remain / shall be' (classical optative/result), and نصیبین is the plural 'shares/portion(s)'. The final cluster (م را) is the enclitic for 'me / to me' (object/possessive), so the phrase means 'the rest which shall remain as my share'.
segments.s576-g28: چو خواهی که = 'if/when you wish that...' (چو here = 'if'); کوته شود is a subjunctive/periphrastic clause 'be shortened' (کوته = short, شود = become / subjunctive 'may be made short'); کین مرا = 'my vengeance' (کین = vengeance, مرا = my/to me). The clause expresses a conditional desire about shortening the speaker's grudge.
segments.s576-g29: برانوش (proper name) is the subject; گفتا is the past verb 'said'. که introduces the reported/content clause. ایران تراست is an archaic possessive/copular structure: تراست = 'is yours' (ترا + است), so 'Iran is yours.'
segments.s576-g30: This is a coordinate nominal predicate: نصیبین ('the shares/portions') و دشت دلیران ('and the plain of the brave' — modifier دلیران follows دشت) followed by تراست ('are yours'). The copula تراست applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s576-g31: پذیرفتم is first‑person past 'I accepted / I take'. این is the demonstrative 'this'. مایه‌ور is an adjective/substantivized adjective 'rich / wealthy' (i.e. 'this wealth'), and باژ و ساو are proper names joined by و. The phrase therefore means 'I accept this wealth (Baj and Sâv).'
segments.s576-g32: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause 'for / because': با + کین و خشمت نداریم = 'we have no vengeance and no anger' (با here 'with/toward', کین 'grudge/vengeance', خشمت 'anger', نداریم present plural 'we do not have'). The final token تاو is an archaic fused pronoun ('تا او' = 'toward him/it'); here it indicates the target of the negated feeling — 'we bear him no grudge or anger.'
segments.s576-g49: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/if'. آید is a present (classical) verb 'comes' (used here to introduce a consequential/future event). ز ما is a prepositional phrase 'from us.' برنگیرد is a negative present/subjunctive-form verb 'does not return / does not turn back' whose subject is سخن; in classical word order the verb can appear before its noun, so here برنگیرد governs the following سخن ('the word/report does not turn back').
segments.s576-g50: نخواهیم is first-person-plural negative/future-ish 'we will not / we do not want (to)'. استا is an archaic form of 'ایستا/ایستادن' meaning 'to stand/abide/stop' — here combined with نخواهیم to mean 'we will not desist/cease/abide'. و is the conjunction 'and', and دین کهن is the noun phrase 'the ancient religion/faith' serving as the complement (what they will not abandon).
segments.s576-g51: زبردست شد is a predicative phrase where the adjective زبردست ('mighty/skillful') precedes the light verb شد 'became' to indicate change of state in the past. مردم زیردست (literally 'the people under-hand') means 'the subordinate people / those beneath (him)', with زیردست functioning as a noun/adjective meaning 'underlings' or 'those under control'.
segments.s576-g52: به کین is a causal/attitudinal prepositional phrase 'out of / in (his) vengeance' where به marks cause. مرد شهری is an indefinite noun phrase 'a city-man / a townsman.' به زین برنشست is a compound past action: the preposition به + زین ('onto the saddle') plus برنشست (classical past 'sat upon / mounted') — so 'he mounted the saddle.'
segments.s576-g53: چو is 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a simple past construction 'information/news came' (آمد = came). به شاپور شاه is the indirect object 'to King Shapur' (the preposition به introduces the recipient); in classical Persian the recipient follows the verb. The whole segment sets the time/circumstance for the reported content that follows.
segments.s576-g54: که introduces reported content 'that'. اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/within'. نصیبین here names the place Nisibis (a place-name rather than the literal senses listed), so the provided lexical senses do not capture its use here. ندادند is past-plural negative 'they did not give/offer' and in this collocation ندادند راه means 'they did not give way / they did not open/pass the road'. راه = 'road/way' completes the phrase.
segments.s576-g55: ز دین ... برآشفت is a causal-expression: ز here means 'because of / from' and takes دین مسیحا 'the Messiah's religion' as its object. برآشفت is a past-form verb 'became angry / was enraged.' شاه is the subject (the king), placed after the verb in classical style.
segments.s576-g56: سپاهی فرستاد is a past simple clause 'he sent troops/an army' (سپاهی here denotes troops/army). بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain adverbial/qualifier in this line (the exact meaning is obsolete in the supplied senses). به راه is the directional phrase 'on/to the road' (به marking direction). The verb فرستاد is third-person past 'sent', so the clause means he dispatched forces along the road.

sentences.s576-s33: At that time they all begged "spare us" and wrote a letter, sending a messenger to the king.
sentences.s576-s34: The renowned king pardoned them and commanded that the army return.
sentences.s576-s35: In every country he won renown; by that same renown he gained success in the world.
sentences.s576-s36: They kept calling him the victorious king; for a while he remained with crown and seat.
sentences.s576-s21: He went rejoicing to Istakhr of Pars, for Istakhr was the pride of Pars upon the earth.
sentences.s576-s22: When they received news at Nisibis, everyone hastened swiftly to the battle.
sentences.s576-s23: For it must not be that King Shapur should seize Nisibis and bring an army.
sentences.s576-s24: For the Christian faith is not true; it is entirely the creed of Zoroaster and the Zand, and so it is.
sentences.s576-s5: All the chiefs came before the king; they came bareheaded and hatless.
sentences.s576-s6: When dinars were thrown before him, they (were) spread out — the old gold was cast far and wide.
sentences.s576-s7: He bestowed (gifts), and Shapur played for them; he well and fittingly set them in order.
sentences.s576-s8: He said to Branoush that from the city of Rome there had come many cruel and ill-omened men.
sentences.s576-s45: He said: "Such is our end; I do not know where our rest will be."
sentences.s576-s46: Some are wholly nasty and foolish; others are endowed with wisdom and refinement.
sentences.s576-s47: This day and that day too will pass; foolish is he who did not entrust the world to the wicked.
sentences.s576-s48: He set his foot on the throne of the Kayanids; for a while he was the world's headman.
sentences.s576-s1: When Branush saw the reply, her pure-hearted heart was swept away with joy.
sentences.s576-s2: He commanded that the famed men of Rome go; a hundred men departed from that border and homeland.
sentences.s576-s3: They loaded dirhams in great quantities, and likewise set out gems and garments.
sentences.s576-s4: From dinars a treasure for offerings was brought forth; thirty thousand (were) raised up from every side.
sentences.s576-s9: Throughout the land of Iran, whatever towns were bad have now entirely turned into nothing but thorny thickets.
sentences.s576-s10: I will make amends for what has been ruined; its place has become the haunt of leopards and lions.
sentences.s576-s11: Branush said, 'What should I say, when you have given a warning not to endure looking at your face?'
sentences.s576-s12: Thus replied the noble king: 'If you wish that I entirely forgive the sin...'
sentences.s576-s41: He had taken from inside Rome whatever treasure there was, hauling it up from every quarter with great toil.
sentences.s576-s42: He brought them and entirely gave them to Shapur; yet for a short while the brim of his hat fluttered full of wind.
sentences.s576-s43: In the end he died in chains and prison, and he handed the Kayan hat over to another.
sentences.s576-s44: King Shapur sent it to Rome — in a coffin and with musk upon the hat's head.
sentences.s576-s29: He kept saying that a prophet who kills Jews and forces his religion upon them ought not to be praised.
sentences.s576-s30: The army set out, circling about like horsemen and lions on the day of battle.
sentences.s576-s31: The fighting there went on for a week; in that city the war made things very tight.
sentences.s576-s32: They slew many of them; they set (their) heads upon the living with heavy fetters.
sentences.s576-s37: The little maid who had freed him had brought him to that fulfillment.
sentences.s576-s38: He named her Dalfrozu Farakh-pish, and among the beauties he made her my Delaram.
sentences.s576-s39: That same gardener had been much sought after; they granted (it) and someone adorned him.
sentences.s576-s40: Caesar remained in prison and fetters, in lamentation and abasement and with wounds from the snare.
sentences.s576-s49: From that time on he sent to the land of the Khuzis much profit and much loss.
sentences.s576-s50: For the prisoners he founded/established one city; he filled the world with bounty from that land.
sentences.s576-s51: Where Khorramabad — a city of ill repute — was; and from that land the joyful abode existed for him.
sentences.s576-s52: Someone whom you had taken earlier (by force) — to this frontier his dwelling belonged; there he took his seat.
sentences.s576-s17: They made a covenant with King Shapur, by which thereafter no host was sent out of Iran.
sentences.s576-s18: Unless with due honor and joy — where shall I go? From him I will lack nothing.
sentences.s576-s19: After that someone acted and struck up music for them, and raised their heads among the renowned.
sentences.s576-s20: When they had departed, the army set out, and he invoked the Creator abundantly.
sentences.s576-s53: His whole realm altogether did him harm; at the New Year they bound a robe upon him.
sentences.s576-s54: He made one into a town and another he set at the evening feast; the victorious Shapur made him renowned by name.
sentences.s576-s55: He made that silver city at Ahvaz; within it he set up palaces and a hospital for him.
sentences.s576-s56: At the side of his captives they gave a name; in that captivity he found sleep and satisfaction.
sentences.s576-s13: From the Roman (Byzantine) dinars he had to give thousands upon thousands three times a year.
sentences.s576-s14: As for the rest that shall be my share — if you wish that my vengeance be shortened.
sentences.s576-s15: Branush said that Iran is yours; the shares and the plain of the brave are yours.
sentences.s576-s16: I accept this wealth — Baj and Sâv — for we bear him no grudge or anger.
sentences.s576-s25: When a message comes from us and the word does not turn back, we will not abandon the ancient faith.
sentences.s576-s26: The subordinates became mighty; a townsman, in vengeance, mounted his saddle.
sentences.s576-s27: When news reached King Shapur that at Nisibis they had not given way.
sentences.s576-s28: The king, angered by the Messiah's religion, sent troops on the road.
